caveats: a: BLS NAICS 336000 includes motor vehicles, aerospace, rail, shipbuilding, and other industries with different capital intensity and occupational mixes.
a: NIST describes manufacturing-wide applications rather than measured task shares for NAICS 336991.
rho: The NIST evidence establishes use cases and adoption barriers, not realized savings in this exact industry.
rho: Realization will vary sharply between manual assemblers, precision component shops, and scaled branded manufacturers.
e: The frozen firm count is itself an estimate and is very small, so one classification decision materially moves the ratio.
e: Harley-Davidson illustrates a transferable operating model but is far above the LMM screen and does not measure the candidate population.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across sectors, not this manufacturing niche.
s5: Survey intentions may not convert to arm's-length control sales within five years.
q: Harley-Davidson's premium brand and dealer network are not representative of a typical LMM parts supplier.
q: PeopleForBikes evidence describes the U.S. bicycle market and not motorcycle or OEM component economics.
d5: Harley-Davidson's premium heavyweight motorcycle results are not the whole motorcycle market.
d5: Participation does not translate one-for-one into purchases of newly manufactured bicycles.
d5: Industry mix can make a specific firm's demand path diverge substantially from the blended range.
o: This is a legal-structural estimate rather than an observed industry statistic.
o: A business may be legally ownable yet economically dependent on nontransferable founder, brand, dealer, or OEM relationships.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Sales of complete vehicles and parts to independent dealers, distributors, OEM customers, fleets, and consumers; repeat revenue can arise from replacement parts, accessories, model refreshes, and recurring OEM or dealer relationships.
lens.excluded_activities: Children's vehicles other than bicycles and metal tricycles, powered golf carts and passenger carriers, pure importers, distributors, retailers, repair shops, and captive operations that are not independently purchasable firms.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers whose primary activity is making motorcycles, bicycles, tricycles, similar equipment, or their parts, including design, fabrication, assembly, finishing, testing, and related aftermarket products.

a.bridge: NIST's cross-manufacturing use cases and BLS's NAICS 336000 occupational mix are mapped to this six-digit industry's workflow. The proxy likely overstates direct task exposure where small plants rely heavily on manual fabrication and assembly, but may understate exposure in design-led or digitally integrated parts firms.
caveats: BLS NAICS 336000 includes motor vehicles, aerospace, rail, shipbuilding, and other industries with different capital intensity and occupational mixes.
NIST describes manufacturing-wide applications rather than measured task shares for NAICS 336991.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-task study from representative motorcycle, bicycle, and component plants, split between production and office/engineering work, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist product and process engineering, demand forecasting, purchasing, scheduling, dealer support, documentation, predictive maintenance, computer-vision quality control, and some robotic programming. Welding, machining setup, painting, physical assembly, road or durability testing, and accountable safety decisions remain substantially physical or human-supervised.

d5.bridge: Recent motorcycle retail contraction and bicycle-market sales commentary are combined with record bicycle participation as directional proxies for five-year real demand. The base assumes partial normalization rather than extrapolating one weak year; the wide range reflects materially different motorcycle, conventional bicycle, e-bike, and parts trajectories.
caveats: Harley-Davidson's premium heavyweight motorcycle results are not the whole motorcycle market.
Participation does not translate one-for-one into purchases of newly manufactured bicycles.
Industry mix can make a specific firm's demand path diverge substantially from the blended range.
d5.change_evidence: Segmented five-year unit, price, installed-base, replacement-cycle, OEM production, inventory, and import data for motorcycles, bicycles, e-bikes, and components would materially narrow the range.
d5.rationale: Demand is discretionary and currently pressured by rates, sentiment, pandemic pull-forward, excess bicycle inventory, and discounting. Replacement parts, maintenance, high bicycle participation, youth engagement, and potential e-bike/category innovation provide a durable installed-base offset.

caveats: The frozen firm count is itself an estimate and is very small, so one classification decision materially moves the ratio.
Harley-Davidson illustrates a transferable operating model but is far above the LMM screen and does not measure the candidate population.
e.change_evidence: A bottom-up census of the seven estimated LMM candidates, including ownership, revenue, primary activity, captive status, and whether operations can transfer independently, would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: The category includes independently owned component makers and niche vehicle manufacturers with plants, employees, customer relationships, and transferable tooling or IP. Exact-screen availability is reduced by captive divisions, large branded companies, import-led sellers, tiny founder-dependent custom shops, and businesses whose economic activity is principally retail or distribution.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336991 ($678,842,905) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336991 ($3,946,662,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $466,840 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336991); class <5: 223 firms, avg revenue/firm $740,126 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $66,907 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 82 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,766,183 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $159,663 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,335,318 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $301,513 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 60 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,720,283 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $969,114 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $50,514,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,566,466 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $356,174,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,198,130 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 7

caveats: This is a legal-structural estimate rather than an observed industry statistic.
A business may be legally ownable yet economically dependent on nontransferable founder, brand, dealer, or OEM relationships.
o.change_evidence: Candidate-level charter documents, cap tables, material contracts, licenses, regulatory approvals, and change-of-control clauses would determine the exact ownable share.
o.rationale: A financially sound buyer can ordinarily own an independent civilian motorcycle, bicycle, or component manufacturer outright. Residual constraints arise from minority interests, dealer or OEM change-of-control provisions, licensed brands or technology, regulated products, lender consents, and occasional foreign-investment or defense-adjacent issues.

caveats: Harley-Davidson's premium brand and dealer network are not representative of a typical LMM parts supplier.
PeopleForBikes evidence describes the U.S. bicycle market and not motorcycle or OEM component economics.
q.change_evidence: Candidate-level gross-margin retention, customer concentration, sole-source status, warranty data, aftermarket mix, price realization, and competitor/import comparisons would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Brands, engineered components, homologation knowledge, tooling, dealer access, and replacement-parts fit can support differentiation and switching costs. Capture is weakened by powerful OEM or dealer customers, abundant imports, model cyclicality, discounting, and high consumer price sensitivity; the bicycle evidence shows participation can rise while new-bike sales remain weak.

rho.bridge: Manufacturing-wide AI applications documented by NIST are translated into the realizable fraction of exposed tasks at small and midsize vehicle and parts plants. Legacy equipment, fragmented data, scarce skills, safety validation, and integration costs push realization below technical exposure; design-led firms may realize more.
caveats: The NIST evidence establishes use cases and adoption barriers, not realized savings in this exact industry.
Realization will vary sharply between manual assemblers, precision component shops, and scaled branded manufacturers.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level pilots reporting adoption cost, error rates, throughput, scrap, downtime, and labor-hour changes over multiple model cycles would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: A buyer can capture meaningful assistance in planning, forecasting, documentation, customer support, engineering iteration, inspection, and maintenance, but brownfield equipment and safety-critical production constrain rapid end-to-end deployment.

s5.bridge: Gallup's all-industry employer-owner succession statistics are used as an availability proxy for private manufacturers. Employer businesses' 22% five-year sell-or-transfer intent anchors the base; aging ownership supports supply, while industrial succession complexity could delay or lower actual marketed transactions.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across sectors, not this manufacturing niche.
Survey intentions may not convert to arm's-length control sales within five years.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed and marketed transactions for U.S. motorcycle, bicycle, and component manufacturers, normalized by the eligible private-company stock, would materially change the value.
s5.rationale: Owner aging should create a steady succession channel, but stated intent is not a completed sale and industry-specific factors such as facility liabilities, tooling finance, dealer relationships, and product liability can inhibit transactions.

principal_driver: Transferable engineered parts, aftermarket fitment, and repeat dealer or OEM relationships can create durable revenue beyond volatile new-vehicle sales.
principal_weakness: A very small estimated target pool combines with discretionary end demand and substantial manual production, limiting both acquisition availability and AI-driven labor leverage.
